  "analysis": "This is a false positive for CWE-079. The reported sink is `rsp.sendRedirect(302, url)` in `ResourceDomainRootAction.Redirection#doDynamic` (core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRootAction.java:150-154). That is an HTTP redirect sink, not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink, so even if attacker input influenced the target URL it would not by itself trigger XSS. The report itself effectively notes this by saying it is \"not a direct XSS sink\". Separately, the concrete taint path from `X-Forwarded-Host` to this redirect is infeasible in the real feature flow: the resource-domain redirect path is only used when the resource-domain feature is configured (`DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:163-166,396-398`), and that configuration requires Jenkins's root URL to already be configured (`ResourceDomainConfiguration.java:95-99,198-205,257-264`). Under that required state, `Jenkins.getRootUrl()` returns the configured root URL immediately (`Jenkins.java:2459-2463`) and does not fall back to `getRootUrlFromRequest()` / `X-Forwarded-Host` (`Jenkins.java:2465-2467,2508-2551`). So the reported source-to-sink path is not reachable here, and the sink is not a CWE-079 sink anyway.",
  "FP_Reason": [

Therefore the branch using `getRootUrlFromRequest()` and `X-Forwarded-Host` cannot be taken in this feature path.",

}",

This is not an XSS sink, but it is a relevant dangerous sink because attacker-controlled data influences class-based deserialization/binding.", "response": "{ "judgement": "FP", "analysis": "The reported dataflow is real, but it does not demonstrate CWE-079. The source is `req.getSubmittedForm()` in `Jenkins.doConfigSubmit`, and the path into `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.configure` is reachable through `configureDescriptor`. However, the reported sink `req.bindJSON(clazz, strategyObject)` is reflective JSON-to-object binding, not HTML/JS output. For CWE-079, attacker-controlled data must reach a browser rendering context without proper escaping. That does not happen on this path. The report itself effectively admits this by saying the sink is 'not an XSS sink' and instead discusses deserialization/binding concerns, which is outside the target CWE. The path is therefore not a true XSS vulnerability. This is not mainly an A1/A2 issue because the call chain is valid; the core mistake is sink/CWE misclassification. As additional context, the handler is protected by `checkPermission(MANAGE)`, so only highly privileged users can submit this configuration, and the only plausible later use of attacker-controlled text (`PatternProjectNamingStrategy.description`) is rendered via Jenkins error views that enable escaping by default.", "FP_Reason": [ "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection" ], "evidence": "Reachable path: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java` `doConfigSubmit(...)` checks `MANAGE`, reads `JSONObject json = req.getSubmittedForm();`, then calls `configureDescriptor(req, json, d)`. In `configureDescriptor`, `JSONObject js = json.has(name) ? json.getJSONObject(name) : new JSONObject(); ... return d.configure(req, js);`. In `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.java`, `configure(...)` does `final JSONObject optJSONObject = json.optJSONObject(\"useProjectNamingStrategy\");`, `final JSONObject strategyObject = optJSONObject.getJSONObject(\"namingStrategy\");`, `final String className = strategyObject.getString(\"$class\");`, `Class clazz = Class.forName(className, true, j.getPluginManager().uberClassLoader);`, and `final ProjectNamingStrategy strategy = (ProjectNamingStrategy) req.bindJSON(clazz, strategyObject);`. This sink only instantiates/binds a `ProjectNamingStrategy`; it does not render HTML. Possible downstream text usage: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/ProjectNamingStrategy.java` throws `new Failure(description ... )` when a job name violates the pattern. But `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Failure.java` stores that string as request attribute `message`, and `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/AbstractModelObject/error.jelly` starts with `` and renders `

${message}

` / `
${message}
`, so HTML is escaped in the error page rather than executed. Therefore user-controlled JSON reaches object binding, not an XSS sink.", "new_reason:": "" }" }

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This report is a false positive. The reported path depends on the `seed` branch in `AbstractItem.getUrl()`, but that branch is not feasible in the `redirectAfterCreateItem(...)` call site. `Functions.getNearestAncestorUrl(req, it)` only returns a value when the current Stapler ancestor list already contains the exact same object instance (`anc.getObject() == it`). In `ItemGroupMixIn.createTopLevelItem`, the `result` item is created during the current `/createItem` request and then immediately redirected; it was not part of request routing, so it cannot already be in `req.getAncestors()`. Therefore the reported `anc.getUrl() -> seed -> substring -> result.getUrl()` flow does not execute here. The reachable branches build only internal Jenkins paths from validated and URL-encoded item/view names. Also, this is not a real CWE-079 sink in practice: the value is used for a redirect target, not rendered into HTML/JavaScript, and the constructed target stays inside the Jenkins application.",
  "FP_Reason": ["A2"],
  "evidence": "1) Exact-object ancestor check makes the reported branch infeasible: `Functions.getNearestAncestorUrl` returns a URL only if `anc.getObject() == it` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/Functions.java:759-764`). 2) In the reported sink path, the item is created and then redirected immediately: `rsp.sendRedirect2(redirectAfterCreateItem(req, result))` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/ItemGroupMixIn.java:209`), and `redirectAfterCreateItem` uses `req.getContextPath() + '/' + result.getUrl() + \"configure\"` (`ItemGroupMixIn.java:228-229`). Because the current request was dispatched to the create endpoint before `result` existed, the new `result` object is not in `req.getAncestors()`. Thus `seed` is null and the reported `seed.substring(...)` branch in `AbstractItem.getUrl()` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/AbstractItem.java:542-546`) is unreachable at this call site. 3) The real reachable URL construction is internal-only: `AbstractItem.getShortUrl()` uses `Util.rawEncode(getName())` (`AbstractItem.java:582-585`), Jenkins root child prefix is `job` (`core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:2439-2441`), and item names are validated by `Jenkins.checkGoodName(name)` before creation (`ItemGroupMixIn.java:168`, `343-345`). `Jenkins.checkGoodName` rejects ISO control characters and `?*/\\\\%!@#$^&|<>[]:;` (`Jenkins.java:4311-4324`), preventing CRLF/header injection and scheme/path tricks. 4) Even the alternative view-specific branch in `AbstractItem.getUrl()` only accepts an ancestor URL if it starts with `req.getContextPath() + \"/\"` (`AbstractItem.java:552-560`), so it also remains within the local app path. Net effect: the actual redirect target is an internal path like `/<context>/job/<encoded-name>/configure`, not attacker-controlled script/HTML and not an off-site redirect.",

}",

Explaination: User-controlled HTTP header is deserialized into a ConsoleAnnotator and then used while streaming HTML output, which can influence rendered content and is a plausible XSS/relevant injection path.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report does not demonstrate a real CWE-079/XSS. The `X-ConsoleAnnotator` header is not reflected as HTML; it is consumed as Jenkins round-trip annotator state in `AnnotatedLargeText.createAnnotator` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/AnnotatedLargeText.java:177-187`) and then used only as a `ConsoleAnnotator` object. When output is rendered, Jenkins escapes ordinary console text before writing it (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/ConsoleAnnotationOutputStream.java:145-148`, `core/src/main/java/hudson/MarkupText.java:301-320`). So arbitrary header text does not become browser-executable content. To turn this path into XSS, an attacker would need to supply a valid recent serialized `ConsoleAnnotator` state that injects markup, not just an arbitrary HTTP header string, and the report does not establish that capability. At most, malformed header values lead to decode/decompression/deserialization errors for that request, not script execution in the browser.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "1) The same class generates the `X-ConsoleAnnotator` response header from the current annotator state in `writeHtmlTo` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/AnnotatedLargeText.java:243-251`), showing this header is intended as server-generated continuation state, not free-form HTML input. 2) The reported sink is not a raw HTML sink for the header value: `ConsoleAnnotationOutputStream` creates `MarkupText`, calls the annotator, and writes `mt.toString(true)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/ConsoleAnnotationOutputStream.java:145-148`); `MarkupText.toString(true)` escapes each plain-text portion with `Util.xmlEscape`/`Util.escape` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/MarkupText.java:301-320`). 3) Markup-capable console notes are trusted structures, not raw log text: `ConsoleNote` adds an HMAC when encoding (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/ConsoleNote.java:212-215`) and rejects unsigned/MAC-mismatched notes when reading (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/ConsoleNote.java:272-276`). 4) Dynamic fields in core annotators are escaped where relevant, e.g. `HyperlinkNote` escapes the URL before inserting markup (`core/src/main/java/hudson/console/HyperlinkNote.java:62-75`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "FP. The reported flow does not implement CWE-079. The source `req.getSubmittedForm()` is used for server-side configuration databinding (`Descriptor.bindJSON` / `req.bindJSON`), not for writing attacker-controlled content into an HTML or JavaScript response. The report text itself says this is \"not XSS\", so it is a CWE mismatch. In addition, the concrete cross-file path in the report is not reachable: `GlobalSecurityConfiguration` only dispatches to descriptors in the `Security` category, while `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration` inherits the default `Unclassified` category and therefore is never called from this security configuration workflow.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`GlobalSecurityConfiguration.configure` enforces `Jenkins.ADMINISTER` before binding (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/GlobalSecurityConfiguration.java:123`). Its relevant operations are typed configuration binds: `Descriptor.bindJSON(req, SecurityRealm.class, ...)` and `Descriptor.bindJSON(req, AuthorizationStrategy.class, ...)` at lines 127-128, plus `req.bindJSON(MarkupFormatter.class, ...)` at line 131. `Descriptor.bindJSON` just delegates to `req.bindJSON(type, src)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Descriptor.java:636-663`); this is object construction/binding, not HTML output, so it is not an XSS sink. The reported hop into `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.configure` is infeasible: the loop at `GlobalSecurityConfiguration.java:147-148` iterates only descriptors matching `FILTER`, and `FILTER` is `input -> input.getCategory() instanceof GlobalConfigurationCategory.Security` (`GlobalSecurityConfiguration.java:189`). `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration` does not override `getCategory()` (`core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.java:39-68`), so it inherits `Descriptor.getCategory()`, which returns `GlobalConfigurationCategory.Unclassified` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Descriptor.java:904-905`). Therefore this descriptor cannot be reached from `GlobalSecurityConfiguration.doConfigure`. Even if invoked directly on its own page, the code binds to a `ProjectNamingStrategy` subtype and casts the result (`GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.java:49-52`), which is expected extension-point configuration rather than XSS.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported dataflow is real and reachable, but it is not a CWE-079/XSS vulnerability. `AbstractAuthenticationToken.getName()` returns the authenticated principal name, `AuthenticationSpringImpl.getName()` forwards it unchanged, and `AccessDeniedException3.reportAsHeadersImpl()` places that value into the HTTP response header `X-You-Are-Authenticated-As`. This header-writing path is executed on real 403 flows, so there is no A1/A2 reachability problem. However, the sink is an HTTP header API (`addHeader`), not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink, so this path does not match CWE-079. At most, it suggests a different class such as HTTP header injection/response splitting (CWE-113). The report’s own explanation explicitly says 'header injection/response splitting rather than XSS', which confirms the CWE mismatch. As an additional practical point, Jenkins’s built-in `HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm` validates local usernames with the default regex `^[\\\\w-]+$`, so CR/LF and typical XSS metacharacters are rejected in the native account-creation flow.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"
  ],
  "evidence": "`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:51-54` returns `principal instanceof UserDetails ? ((UserDetails) principal).getUsername() : String.valueOf(principal)`. `core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.java:69-71` returns `delegate.getName()`. The actual reported sink is `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`: `rsp.addHeader(\"X-You-Are-Authenticated-As\", authentication.getName());`. That method is called from `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java:52-53` and `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonAuthenticationEntryPoint.java:95-99`, so the path is reachable in 403 handling. But `addHeader(...)` writes an HTTP header, not page content or script. For the built-in realm, `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:123` defines `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = \"^[\\\\w-]+$\"`, and `validateAccountCreationForm` rejects usernames that fail this check (`lines 429-435`, `509-513`), so CR/LF needed for response splitting are not accepted through Jenkins’s native signup path.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:54-54: getUsername(...) : String

{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported flow is real, but it is not a real-world CWE-079 vulnerability in Jenkins. `DirectoryBrowserSupport` does read a string and place it into `Content-Security-Policy` response headers, but the value comes only from trusted deployment configuration: a JVM system property or a servlet `init-param`. Those inputs are controlled by the Jenkins administrator/deployer, not by an external attacker or ordinary Jenkins user. So the report does not show an attacker-controlled source reaching an XSS sink. The path is reachable, but the source is misidentified for vulnerability purposes. This is best classified as an FP due to incorrect source selection (B1). A secondary issue is that the sink is CSP-header configuration, not direct HTML/JavaScript output, so the report also does not cleanly match CWE-079.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`jenkins.util.SystemProperties$Listener` is registered as a servlet listener in `core/src/main/resources/META-INF/web-fragment.xml:172`. In `core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/SystemProperties.java:105-116`, the listener sets `handler = key -> theContext.getInitParameter(key)`. In `SystemProperties.getString` (`core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/SystemProperties.java:235-250`), Jenkins first reads `System.getProperty(key)` and then `handler.getString(key)`, so the only inputs are JVM system properties or servlet init parameters. In `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:107`, the property name is fixed to `hudson.model.DirectoryBrowserSupport.CSP`; then `DirectoryBrowserSupport` reads it at `:402` and writes it to CSP headers at `:405-406`. This is an intentional admin escape hatch: `test/src/test/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupportTest.java:332-343` explicitly does `System.setProperty(propName, \"\")` and verifies the CSP headers disappear, and `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRecommendation.java:57-64` activates an administrative warning when this property is overridden. The message in `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRecommendation/message.properties` says overriding `hudson.model.DirectoryBrowserSupport.CSP` is a 'potential security issue when browsing untrusted files', which confirms Jenkins treats this as deliberate administrator configuration, not attacker input. Therefore there is no remotely exploitable XSS path here; an attacker would already need deployment/admin control to set the source value.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/SystemProperties.java:110-110: getInitParameter(...) : String

Explaination: Servlet init parameter input can be externally configured and flows into HTTP response headers. This is header injection / security policy manipulation rather than direct XSS, but it is a relevant vulnerability path."

Explaination: User-controlled principal name flows into an HTTP response header. This is header injection/response splitting risk rather than XSS, and the source is a public/auth-derived value that may contain attacker-controlled input.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported flow is reachable, but it is not a CWE-079/XSS vulnerability. `AbstractAuthenticationToken.getName()` returns the principal/username (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:52-54`), `AuthenticationSpringImpl.getName()` delegates it (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.java:69-71`), and `ACL.checkPermission` throws `AccessDeniedException3` on permission failure (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/ACL.java:71-80`). The exception is then rendered by the denial handler, which calls `reportAsHeaders` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java:48-54`). The actual sink is `rsp.addHeader(\"X-You-Are-Authenticated-As\", authentication.getName())` in `AccessDeniedException3` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`). That is an HTTP response header sink, not an HTML/JavaScript sink, so it does not satisfy CWE-079. This is therefore not an A2 unreachable-path case; the path is real. The decisive error is B1: incorrect sink/CWE selection. At most, if unsafe usernames with CR/LF were accepted, this would be a header-injection/response-splitting style issue rather than XSS. For default Jenkins accounts, the built-in `HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm` also restricts usernames with `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = \"^[\\\\w-]+$\"` and rejects invalid characters during signup, reducing even that alternate risk in the default realm.",

((UserDetails) principal).getUsername() : String.valueOf(principal);` (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:52-54`). Reachability: `ACL.checkPermission` throws `new AccessDeniedException3(a, p)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/ACL.java:76-80`), and `AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.handle` invokes `((AccessDeniedException3) cause).reportAsHeaders(rsp)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java:52-53`). Actual sink: `rsp.addHeader(\"X-You-Are-Authenticated-As\", authentication.getName())` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`). Regression test confirms the value is exposed as an HTTP header, not page content: `assertEquals(\"user\", x.getResponse().getResponseHeaderValue(\"X-You-Are-Authenticated-As\"))` (`test/src/test/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3Test.java:97-100`). Built-in realm validation: `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = \"^[\\\\w-]+$\"` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:123`) and signup rejects invalid usernames (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:431-435`).",

}"

The reported sink is `StaplerResponse2.sendRedirect(302, url)` in `ResourceDomainRootAction.Redirection.doDynamic`, which creates an HTTP redirect, not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink, so it does not satisfy CWE-079. The report itself even states this is \"open redirect/header-injection, not XSS\", which confirms the CWE mismatch. Separately, the specific `X-Forwarded-Host -> getRootUrlFromRequest() -> sendRedirect` path is not feasible in Jenkins: `Jenkins.getRootUrl()` only falls back to `getRootUrlFromRequest()` when the configured root URL is null, but the resource-domain flow that reaches `ResourceDomainRootAction` only runs when `ResourceDomainConfiguration.isResourceDomainConfigured()` is true, and that method requires a non-null configured Jenkins root URL.",

  "evidence": "`core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRootAction.java` lines 150-154 build `String url = Jenkins.get().getRootUrl() + this.url + restOfPath;` and then call `rsp.sendRedirect(302, url);`; this is a redirect/Location-header sink, not a browser-side script execution sink. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java` lines 2459-2467 show `getRootUrl()` returns `config.getUrl()` when configured, and only calls `getRootUrlFromRequest()` otherwise. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java` lines 2508-2551 show `getRootUrlFromRequest()` is where `X-Forwarded-Host` is read. `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java` lines 164-166 only enables the resource-domain token flow when `ResourceDomainConfiguration.isResourceDomainConfigured()` is true. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainConfiguration.java` makes `isResourceDomainConfigured()` false when `JenkinsLocationConfiguration.get().getUrl()` is null, so the redirect flow is disabled exactly when `getRootUrl()` would have used header-derived data. Jenkins' own test `test/src/test/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainTest.java` method `clearRootUrl` sets the root URL to null and verifies the response is no longer served via the resource domain (`static-files`/second-domain redirect disappears).",

Explaination: User-controlled JSON from req.getSubmittedForm() reaches req.bindJSON(clazz, strategyObject), enabling attacker-influenced object binding. This is not XSS specifically, but it is a relevant unsafe deserialization/object-instantiation style sink.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This report is a false positive for CWE-079. The source `req.getSubmittedForm()` is user-controlled JSON, but the selected sink `req.bindJSON(clazz, strategyObject)` in `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.configure` is not an HTML/JavaScript output sink; it is Stapler data binding used to instantiate/configure a `ProjectNamingStrategy`. Even if data reaches `bindJSON`, that does not by itself create XSS, so the sink is misidentified for CWE-079 (B1). The reported cross-file path is also not runtime-feasible as written: `AppearanceGlobalConfiguration` only dispatches descriptors in `AppearanceCategory`, while `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration` does not override `getCategory()` and therefore remains in the default `GlobalConfigurationCategory.Unclassified`. Its real entry point is `Jenkins.doConfigSubmit`, not the appearance page flow (A2/D3). The report explanation itself also admits this is \"not XSS specifically\" and reframes it as object binding, which further deviates from the target CWE definition (C1). In real use, malformed input here could at most cause an admin-side configuration/binding error, not a demonstrated XSS issue.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`AppearanceGlobalConfiguration` dispatches only appearance descriptors: `FILTER = input -> input.getCategory() instanceof AppearanceCategory;` and `for (Descriptor<?> d : Functions.getSortedDescriptorsForGlobalConfigByDescriptor(FILTER)) { result &= configureDescriptor(req, json, d); }`. `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration` does not override `getCategory()`, and `Descriptor.getCategory()` defaults to `GlobalConfigurationCategory.Unclassified`, so this descriptor cannot be reached through the appearance flow. The real unclassified global-config path is in `Jenkins.doConfigSubmit`: `for (Descriptor<?> d : Functions.getSortedDescriptorsForGlobalConfigUnclassified()) result &= configureDescriptor(req, json, d);`. The alleged sink is `final ProjectNamingStrategy strategy = (ProjectNamingStrategy) req.bindJSON(clazz, strategyObject);` in `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.java:52`; this binds JSON into a `ProjectNamingStrategy` and stores it via `j.setProjectNamingStrategy(strategy)`. No HTML/JS rendering sink is shown in the reported path. The corresponding UI uses form controls (`f.descriptorRadioList`, `f.textbox`) in `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/model/GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration/config.groovy` and `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/model/ProjectNamingStrategy/PatternProjectNamingStrategy/config.groovy`, while Jenkins views here use `<?jelly escape-by-default='true'?>`, e.g. `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/appearance/AppearanceGlobalConfiguration/index.jelly`. Access is also restricted to privileged users: `Jenkins.doConfigSubmit` checks `MANAGE`, `AppearanceGlobalConfiguration.configure` calls `j.checkPermission(Jenkins.MANAGE)`, and `GlobalProjectNamingStrategyConfiguration.getRequiredGlobalConfigPagePermission()` returns `Jenkins.MANAGE`.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
7:
{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported data flow is real, but it does not constitute CWE-079. `req.getQueryString()` is user-controlled, and it is appended to a redirect target, but the sink is the HTTP `Location` header, not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink. The code is implementing Jenkins' internal two-step authentication redirect (`/original` -> `/secured/original` -> `/original`). That may be relevant to redirect/header-handling reviews in general, but it is not reflected XSS by itself. The path is straightforward and reachable under specific authentication conditions, so this is not mainly an A1/A2 issue; the key problem is incorrect sink/vulnerability classification (B1). As a secondary observation, the report text itself admits this is 'not a direct XSS sink', which also shows misalignment with CWE-079.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "In `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:171-188`, Jenkins constructs `path = req.getContextPath() + \"/secured\" + path;`, then copies the query string with `String q = req.getQueryString(); if (q != null) path += '?' + q;`, and finally writes `rsp.setHeader(\"Location\", path)`. In `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4365-4370`, the `/secured` handler redirects back with `String path = req.getContextPath() + req.getOriginalRestOfPath();` plus the same query string, followed by `rsp.sendRedirect2(path)`. In both places, attacker input is only used as part of a URL for browser navigation; there is no HTML body output, no template rendering, no DOM/JS sink, and no server-side decoding of the query string into executable content. A payload such as `<script>alert(1)</script>` would remain part of the URL, not execute as script. The redirect target is also anchored to Jenkins paths via `req.getContextPath() + \"/secured\" + ...` / `req.getContextPath() + req.getOriginalRestOfPath()`, so the query string does not make this an attacker-chosen XSS target.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:172-172: getQueryString(...) : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:177-177: path : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:186-186: path : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:188-188: path

Explaination: User-controlled query string flows into the HTTP Location header in a redirect. This is not a direct XSS sink, but it is a relevant header/redirect injection issue if untrusted input is placed in the header without sanitization."
}
